reasoning: The finding is confirmed real. hidedflipRating() at line 130-134 is registered via add_action('wp_ajax_hidedflipRating', ...) at line 91 inside an is_admin() guard. During admin-ajax.php requests WordPress sets is_admin()=true unconditionally, so the guard does not restrict to privileged users — any logged-in user (subscriber, customer) can POST to admin-ajax.php with action=hidedflipRating. The handler has no current_user_can() check and no nonce/check_ajax_referer call. The sink update_option('dflip_showratingdiv','no') writes to the wp_options table with hardcoded key and value — no attacker-controlled data flows into the call, so there is no injection risk. The effect is permanently suppressing the plugin's own 'please rate us' admin notice. Reachability and auth claims are correct: wp_ajax_ (not wp_ajax_nopriv_) requires a logged-in session, but any role suffices. Severity is low, not medium: the only impact is cosmetic UI suppression of a rating banner visible only to admins; no data is exposed, no privilege is escalated, and no site content or security configuration is altered.
